title: GANs Recap
concept: GANs pit a generator against a discriminator in an adversarial game, producing sharp results through competition rather than direct reconstruction (Units 201-220).

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym GAN stand for?
exercises.explain: GANs stand for Generative Adversarial Networks, where two networks compete in an adversarial setup to generate new data.

cards.title: The Ultimate Artificial Rivalry
cards.body: How do computer algorithms learn to create incredibly realistic images of things that don't exist? Instead of just copying patterns, they learn through an epic, head-to-head competition!

cards.title: What is a GAN?
cards.body: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) consist of two neural networks: a Generator that creates fake data, and a Discriminator that tries to distinguish fake data from real data. Their competition forces the generated data to look incredibly realistic.

cards.title: The Art Detective and the Forger
cards.body: Imagine an art student trying to paint a fake masterpiece, and an art detective trying to spot the fraud. The student gets better only because the detective keeps pointing out the flaws. They push each other to be perfect.

cards.title: GANs in Action
cards.body: Modern photo-editing apps use GANs to turn rough doodles into highly detailed landscape photos, or to generate realistic human faces of people who do not actually exist.

cards.title: The Generator Never Sees Real Images
cards.body: You might think the Generator has access to the database of real images, but it doesn't! The Generator only gets feedback from the Discriminator. It learns entirely from the detective's critiques.

cards.title: Generator vs. Discriminator
cards.body: Compare the roles of the two main components in a Generative Adversarial Network.

left.text: Takes random noise and tries to paint a realistic image to fool the judge.

right.text: Examines both real and fake images to decide which ones are authentic.

exercises.prompt: How do GANs achieve highly detailed and sharp results?

options.text: By copying and pasting pixels from real images.
Through an adversarial competition between two networks.
By using a single network that runs backwards.
By averages of all images in the dataset.

exercises.explain: GANs produce sharp results because of the competitive, adversarial game between the Generator and the Discriminator, rather than using direct pixel-by-pixel reconstruction.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The Generator starts out producing very poor quality images (noise) and refines them over time.
exercises.explain: The Generator starts by producing random noise and gradually improves as the Discriminator gets better at spotting its mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing network name in this GAN setup:
exercises.sentence: The Generator creates fake images, while the [blank] tries to detect if they are fake.
exercises.explain: The Discriminator acts as the judge or detective, analyzing images to determine if they are real or generated fakes.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following tasks is a GAN designed to do?

options.text: Make a photo of a cat
Where is the cat in this photo?

exercises.explain: The prompt 'Make a photo of a cat' asks the AI to generate new data from scratch, which is the primary purpose of a Generative Adversarial Network. The other options are classification and regression tasks.

exercises.prompt: In a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), how do the two internal networks interact?

options.text: The Generator evaluates the real images to copy them directly.
Both networks work together using the exact same loss function.
The Generator creates images from noise while the Discriminator tries to spot the fakes.
The Discriminator creates the images and the Generator rates them.

exercises.explain: In a GAN, the Generator creates images from random noise, while the Discriminator tries to classify images as real or fake. They do not share a single loss function; instead, they compete against each other in an adversarial game.
